1: to de pneuma rētōs legei hoti en hysterois kairois apostēsontai tines tēs pisteōs — 'the Spirit explicitly says that in later times some will depart from the faith.' Rētōs (explicitly, in so many words) — the Spirit's utterance is clear and specific, not vague. Whether this refers to a specific prophecy received in the community or to prophetic texts from the OT/Jesus tradition is unclear. Hysterois kairois (later times) — the same eschatological category as eschatais hēmerais (last days) in 2 Tim 3:1. Paul applies this future warning to the present Ephesian situation: the 'later times' are now. Apostēsontai tēs pisteōs (will depart from the faith) — the apostasia of 2 Thess 2:3 at the individual level.
2: en hypokrisei pseudologōn kekautēriasmenōn tēn idian suneidēsin — 'through the hypocrisy of liars whose own consciences are seared.' Kekautēriasmenōn (seared, branded with a hot iron) — the perfect passive participle indicates a completed action with ongoing results: their consciences were seared and remain deadened. The branding metaphor suggests permanent damage to the moral sensory apparatus. The hypokrisei (hypocrisy) connects to the false teachers' public presentation vs. their internal state: they perform spiritual authority while their consciences are cauterized.
3: kōluontōn gamein, apechesthai brōmatōn — 'forbidding marriage, commanding abstinence from foods.' The two ascetic prohibitions are the concrete content of the false teaching. In Jewish thought, both marriage and food were created goods; the false teachers' prohibition of them implies a negative view of the material order. Paul's counter: ha ho theos ektisen eis metalēmpsin meta eucharistias (which God created to be received with thanksgiving) — creation is the ground of the refutation. What God created as good cannot be intrinsically defiling. The eschatological language: the error of these teachers treats the material world as the enemy of spiritual progress.
4: hoti pan ktisma theou kalon kai ouden apoblēton — 'for everything God created is good and nothing is to be rejected.' The Pauline creatio ex nihilo affirmation has ethical consequences: the goodness of creation establishes the goodness of what is created. Apoblēton (to be rejected, thrown away) — the food-purity laws of Judaism and the asceticism of the false teachers are both put under the theological criterion of creation: if God made it, rejecting it without warrant contradicts the Creator's assessment.
5: hagiazetai gar dia logou theou kai enteuxeōs — 'for it is sanctified by the word of God and prayer.' Hagiazetai (is sanctified, made holy) — the present passive indicates an ongoing process, not a one-time event. The mechanism of sanctification: logos theou (word of God — the creation narrative of Gen 1 which calls everything good, and possibly the blessing prayer over food) + enteuxis (intercession/prayer). The blessing of food before eating is thus theologically significant: it enacts the acknowledgment that what God created is good, reversing the false teachers' implicit rejection of creation.
6: tauta hypotitheios tois adelphois kalos esē diakonos Christou Iēsou — 'by pointing these things out to the brothers you will be a good servant of Christ Jesus.' Hypotitheios (putting under, suggesting, laying before) — a gentle rather than authoritative verb: Timothy is not commanded to enforce but to set before the community. Enentrapheis tois logois tēs pisteōs kai tēs kalēs didaskalias hē parēkolouthēkas (nourished on the words of the faith and the good teaching you have followed) — the metaphor: spiritual nourishment from doctrinal content is what equips for ministry, paralleling physical nourishment.
7: tous de bebēlous kai graōdeis muthous paraitou — 'have nothing to do with irreverent, silly myths.' Bebēlous (profane, worldly — the opposite of hagios/holy) characterizes the myths as without sacred substance. Graōdeis (old-womanish — a Hellenistic put-down for superstitious, credulous, uncritical beliefs; cf. Plutarch's use of the term for folk superstitions) is blunt condescension toward the false teaching. Gymnaze de seauton pros eusebeian (train yourself for godliness) — gymnaō is athletic training: the discipline required for godliness is the same habituating, repeated effort as athletic conditioning.
8: hē gar sōmatikē gymnasia pros oligon estin ōphelimos — 'for physical training is of some value.' The concessive acknowledgment of physical training's value (pros oligon ōphelimos — useful for a little, to a limited degree) makes the comparison fair rather than dismissive. Hē de eusebeia pros panta ōphelimos estin (but godliness is beneficial for all things) — the contrast is scope: physical training benefits the present life only; godliness (eusebeia) benefits both present and future life (epangelian echousa zōēs tēs nun kai tēs mellousēs — holding promise of life both now and coming). The temporal contrast (now vs. the age to come) is the eschatological ground for prioritizing godliness.
9: pistos ho logos kai pasēs apodochēs axios — 'trustworthy is the saying and worthy of full acceptance.' The third occurrence of the pistos ho logos formula in 1 Timothy. The saying it endorses is either v.8 (godliness has promise for both lives) or v.10 (we labor because we hope in the living God) — the formula works in both directions. The repetition of the formula within one letter signals that Paul is marking recognized community affirmations, not merely his personal opinions.
10: eis touto gar kopiōmen kai agonizometha hoti ēlpikamen epi theō zōnti — 'for to this end we toil and strive, because we have set our hope on the living God.' Kopiōmen (we toil — present tense, ongoing) and agonizometha (we strive/agonize — the athletic contest verb) describe apostolic ministry as sustained effort requiring hope as its motivating foundation. Ēlpikamen (we have set our hope, perfect tense — with ongoing effect) — the settled nature of the hope is what makes sustained labor possible. Sōtēr pantōn anthrōpōn malista pistōn (Savior of all people, especially of those who believe) — the 'especially' (malista) distinguishes the mode of salvation: God is the Savior of all in some sense (providential preservation) but especially of those who believe in the soteriological sense.
11: paraggelle tauta kai didaske — 'command and teach these things.' The two verbs paraggelle (command, give orders — the military/authoritative term Paul uses for firm directives) and didaske (teach — the pedagogical term) pair authority with instruction: Timothy is to both assert the standard and explain it. The brevity of this imperative sentence after the extended theological argument (vv. 1-10) signals a return to direct address: the teaching must now be enacted.
12: mēdeis sou tēs neotētos kataphroneito — 'let no one despise your youth.' Kataphroneō (despise, look down on, treat with contempt) — the present imperative with negative forbids an ongoing pattern: opponents were already dismissing Timothy on grounds of youth. Neotēs (youth) — Timothy was probably in his 30s at the time, young for a significant leadership role by ancient standards where age carried authority. The counter-command: alla tupos ginou tōn pistōn (but become a pattern/example for the believers) — the word tupos (type, pattern, model) means a visible impression that others can imitate.
13: heōs erchomai prosece tē anagnōsei, tē paraklēsei, tē didaskaliā — 'until I come, devote yourself to the public reading of Scripture, to exhortation, to teaching.' The three activities form the synagogue-liturgy structure that early Christian worship inherited: anagnōsis (public reading of Scripture — the weekly Torah-and-Prophets reading), paraklēsis (exhortation — homiletical application), didaskalia (teaching — systematic doctrinal instruction). The construction 'until I come' makes these activities the regular ministry of Timothy's ongoing presence, not extraordinary measures.
14: mē amelei tou en soi charismatos ho edothē soi dia prophēteias meta epitheseōs tōn cheirōn tou presbyteriou — 'do not neglect the spiritual gift within you, which was given you through prophecy with the laying on of hands of the elders.' Amelei (neglect, be careless about) — the present imperative negative: Timothy is becoming negligent. The charism was given through two mechanisms: prophetic designation (dia prophēteias — a prophetic word identified Timothy as called) and the bodily act of the elders' hand-laying. The gift is therefore both divinely conferred and communally confirmed — not self-appointed or individually claimed.
15: tauta meleta, en toutois isthi hina sou hē prokopē phanera ē pasin — 'practice these things, be in them, so that your progress may be evident to all.' Meleta (practice, attend to, study) — the present imperative of ongoing discipline; en toutois isthi (be in these things) — total immersion in the ministry content. Prokopē (progress, advancement) — a Stoic term for moral and intellectual growth. The expectation that Timothy's growth will be phanera pasin (evident/visible to all) makes pastoral development a public, observable process — leadership formation happens in community view.
16: epimene autois; touto gar poiōn kai seauton sōseis kai tous akouontas sou — 'persist in them; for by doing this you will save both yourself and your hearers.' Epimene (persist, continue in) — the present tense of ongoing faithfulness. The final clause — sōseis kai seauton kai tous akouontas (you will save both yourself and your hearers) — is striking: Timothy's persistent faithfulness in doctrine and life is instrumental to salvation. This does not contradict justification by faith but asserts that the teacher's continuance in truth is the means through which ongoing saving knowledge reaches others.
